name: DNA Damage Response and Repair
summary: The DNA damage response (DDR) pathway detects and repairs DNA lesions to maintain genomic integrity. Double-strand breaks activate ATM/ATR kinases, which phosphorylate effectors including p53 (TP53). TP53 induces cell cycle arrest, DNA repair, or apoptosis. BRCA1 and BRCA2 function in homologous recombination repair; mutations cause hereditary breast and ovarian cancer. BRCA1/2 proteins stabilize stalled replication forks and promote error-free repair. TP53 mutations are common in cancers and Li-Fraumeni syndrome. UV radiation and endogenous replication stress trigger the pathway. EGFR signaling can suppress apoptosis and interact with DDR. Defective repair leads to mutation accumulation and cancer.

environmental_triggers.trigger_type: Direct DNA damage; cyclobutane dimers and 6-4 photoproducts activate DDR
Carcinogens cause adducts and double-strand breaks; oxidative stress
Oxidative stress and DNA damage; arsenic is carcinogenic

key_genes.role_in_pathway: Homologous recombination; DNA end resection; replication fork protection; tumor suppressor
Recruits RAD51 for strand invasion; homologous recombination; replication fork stability
Transcription factor; cell cycle arrest, DNA repair, apoptosis; guardian of genome
Growth signaling; can suppress apoptosis; mutations common in lung cancer

tissue_specificity.expression_evidence: EGFR and TP53 in lung cancer

linked_diseases.pathway_role: BRCA1/2 mutations cause hereditary breast cancer; pathway defects common in sporadic cases
TP53 mutations and mismatch repair defects; chromosomal instability
TP53 and EGFR mutations; tobacco-induced damage

linked_exposures.pathway_effect: UV causes lesions requiring nucleotide excision repair; TP53 mutations in skin cancer
Estrogenic compounds may interact with BRCA pathway in breast cancer

references.title: BRCA1 and BRCA2 in DNA repair
Hereditary breast cancer: BRCA and beyond
